data.title: Enhance fix-bug skill: anti-pattern scan, flaky scoring, and agent separation
data.description:
## Context

When a bug is fixed in one location, the same anti-pattern often exists elsewhere in the codebase. Today, CLAUDE.md contains guidance to "search for the same anti-pattern elsewhere" (Always Do These, rule 9), but this is advisory — the fix-bug skill has no mandatory step enforcing it. This leads to repeated bug-fix cycles for the same root cause in different files (as demonstrated by the relative-path bug in 3 plugin scripts). Additionally, the current scoring rubric lacks a dimension for intermittent/flaky bugs, which require deeper investigation due to non-deterministic reproduction. Finally, the skill allows the same agent context to both diagnose and fix a bug, creating confirmation bias risk — the agent that proposed a root cause is naturally inclined to believe its own fix addresses it.

## Success Criteria

1. After a fix is verified GREEN, the fix-bug skill performs a mandatory codebase scan for other instances of the confirmed root cause pattern. For each candidate file identified by the scan, a RED test reproducing the original bug pattern against that file is written and confirmed failing, the fix is applied, and the test is confirmed passing. Candidates in the same file are handled by a single sub-agent in one pass. If a candidate pattern spans two files (e.g., a call site and its shared utility), both files are assigned to the same sub-agent batch slot. Fix sub-agents are dispatched in batches of up to 5, with a commit (following the standard commit workflow including review) between batches. If the scan returns zero confirmed candidates, the scan step records an explicit empty-scan result and the skill proceeds to commit normally.
2. The scoring rubric includes an "intermittent/flaky" dimension scored 0-2, additive to the existing complexity score total. 0 = reproduction is deterministic across 3 consecutive runs. 1 = at least one of: CI history shows intermittent failures on this path, reproduction requires a specific environment variable or timing condition, or reproduction succeeds less than 100% of the time in the investigator's runs. 2 = the investigator directly observed a failure on one run and a pass on an identical subsequent run. The dimension is a straight additive contribution; tier thresholds are not recalibrated.
3. For behavioral bugs (root cause requires reasoning about runtime state, LLM output, or logic flow), the agent that performs root cause investigation (Step 2) is never the same agent that implements the fix (Step 6). Structural enforcement: the fix step requires a root_cause_report input produced by a prior sub-agent invocation via the Task tool — the orchestrating agent may not produce this field itself. A reviewer can verify this by confirming the SKILL.md fix step contains an explicit assertion that root_cause_report originated from a named sub-agent tool call.
4. The mechanical fix path (root cause is a syntax error, import error, config syntax violation, or type annotation error that routes through the existing mechanical fast-path) and bot-psychologist are explicitly exempted from the agent separation requirement.
5. The fix-bug skill's commit step includes an explicit pre-condition check: if the anti-pattern scan returned one or more confirmed candidates, the skill reads the batch completion record and verifies every candidate entry has status GREEN before invoking the commit workflow. If any entry is not GREEN, the skill halts and surfaces the incomplete candidates to the user. This check is a named step in SKILL.md.
6. After the epic ships, the epic ticket remains open until 5 bug fix session observations are recorded as ticket comments. Each observation must note (a) whether the mandatory scan surfaced at least one confirmed candidate, and (b) whether a conclusion divergence between diagnosing and fixing agents was observed. The criterion passes when all 5 observation comment records exist on the epic ticket, regardless of findings. A separate tracking ticket is created at epic ship time to own the observation collection so sprint closure is not blocked.

## Scenario Analysis

- Anti-pattern scan sub-agent batch exceeds context window on large codebases (high, Runtime): Scan agent silently truncates search, orchestrator treats partial list as complete
- Flaky-bug investigation timeout causes premature mechanical-path fallback (high, Runtime): Timeout on flaky investigation causes silent bypass of agent separation via mechanical path exemption
- Parallel fix sub-agents produce conflicting edits (high, Runtime): Two sub-agents modify same shared utility, causing working tree conflicts
- Agent-separation hard gate misclassifies behavioral bugs with mixed mechanical signals (high, Runtime): Bug with behavioral root cause and mechanical symptom routes to mechanical exemption
- Review gate blocks anti-pattern batch commits (critical, Deployment): Each batch commit must follow the standard commit workflow including review
- Flaky score inflates tier routing thresholds (medium, Configuration): New dimension inflates total scores — mitigated by straight additive contribution without threshold recalibration
- Bot-psychologist exemption absorbs all behavioral bugs (high, Configuration): Classification boundary must be tightly scoped to bot-psychologist's explicit routing criteria
- Anti-pattern scan has no configurable scope bound (high, Configuration): Scan should respect source-file scope exclusions (test files, vendored code, fixtures)
- Zero confirmed instances blocks commit indefinitely (medium, Configuration): Explicit empty-result exit condition defined in SC-1

## Dependencies

Potential interaction with "Extract review and evaluation sub-agents into dedicated plugin agents" (dso-8l5h): if that epic restructures sub-agent dispatch patterns, SC-3's structural constraint may need alignment. Not a blocking dependency — both can proceed independently, but changes should be coordinated if worked in the same sprint.

## Approach

Three changes to the fix-bug SKILL.md, prioritized: (1) Anti-pattern scan step (SC-1, SC-5) — primary outcome, highest user value. (2) Agent separation enforcement (SC-3, SC-4) — structural improvement. (3) Flaky scoring dimension (SC-2) — rubric addition. All changes target the same file and share the theme of improving fix-bug rigor.

### Planning Intelligence Log

- Web research (Step 2.6): not triggered
  - Bright-line conditions that fired: none
- Scenario analysis (Step 2.75): triggered
  - Scenarios surviving blue team filter: 9
- Practitioner-requested additional cycles: none
